coredata.dc:description: Photothermal materials, especially photothermal plastics, are crucial building blocks for functional devices. Covalently immobilizing the photothermal carbon dots in plastic matrix is a promising method for producing efficient photothermal plastics. However, the reactive moieties of photothermal carbon dots are often destroyed because of harsh preparation conditions, preventing their covalent interaction with plastic matrix. Here, we conceptualized carbon dots with photocaged reactivity (P-CDs) for producing ultra-stable photothermal plastics. During the formation of P-CDs, hydroxyl moieties were maintained in the preparation environment. Upon UV irradiation, hydroxyl moieties of P-CDs were in situ converted into aldehyde groups and reacted with amino groups in the polysaccharide matrix, producing P-CD plastics. As-obtained P-CD plastics showed strong stability against solution immersion and UV aging. In particular, the P-CD plastics showed a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 46.6%. Such efficient and robust photothermal P-CD plastics were further applied to prepare a solar-driven thermoelectric generator (TEG) for energy generation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of P-CDs, P-CD plastic, and the application of P-CD plastic
(A) Preparation of carbon dots with photocaged reactivity (P-CDs).
(B) Preparation of P-CD plastics.
(C) Application of P-CD plastic in solar-driven thermoelectric generators (TEGs).
The characterization, photothermal conversion performance, and schematic mechanism of P-CDs
(A) HR-TEM images of P-CDs.
(B) XPS survey spectrum of P-CDs.
(C) Time-dependent changes in absorbance spectra of P-CDs (5 μg/mL aqueous solution) under UV light irradiation.
(D) FT-IR spectra of P-CDs before and after 20 min UV irradiation.
(E) The photo-kinetic plots of the in situ hydroxyl-to-aldehyde conversion of P-CDs. The content of aldehyde groups (ALD %) was used to investigate conversion efficiency.
(F) Temperature changes of P-CDs before and after UV irradiation upon artificial solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2).
(G) Fluorescence of P-CDs before and after 20 min UV irradiation.

(H) Schematic illustration of mechanism of solar-to-thermal effect of P-CDs and the increase of photothermal conversion efficiency after UV irradiation (G, ground state; S, excited state). See also Figures S1–S11 and Schemes S1 and S2.
Characteristics of P-CD plastics
(A, C, and D) Digital images of (A) P-CD plastics, (C) non-crosslinked P-CD plastics, and (D) control plastics and the corresponding schematic microstructures.
(B) AFM topography of a typical P-CD plastic.

(E–H) Mechanical properties of control plastics, non-crosslinked P-CD plastics, and P-CD plastics. (E) Stress-strain curves, (F) tensile strength, strain to failure, (G) toughness, and (H) Young’s moduli.
(I) Tensile strengths and Young’s moduli of P-CD plastic compared with currently widely used plastics.

(J) UV-vis spectra showing the absorbance of P-CDs after immersing P-CD plastics (2 cm × 1 cm × 60 μm), non-crosslinked P-CD plastics (2 cm × 1 cm × 60 μm), GO-HA plastic (2 cm × 1 cm × 60 μm), and CNT-HA plastic (2 cm × 1 cm × 60 μm) in water/ethanol (v/v = 1/1). See also Figures S12–S14, Schemes S3 and S4, and Table S1.
The photothermal conversion performance of P-CD plastics
(A) Absorbance of P-CD plastic, non-crosslinked P-CD plastic, and control plastic overlaid on the solar spectrum.
(B) Temperature changes of P-CD plastic, non-crosslinked P-CD plastic, and control plastic irradiated with simulated solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2).
(C) Time development of infrared images of P-CD plastic under different irradiation intensities with artificial solar (2 sun: 200 mW cm−2; 3 sun: 300 mW cm−2). Scale bar, 1 cm.
(D) Temperature changes of P-CD plastic with different concentrations of P-CDs irradiated with artificial solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2).
(E) Temperature changes of P-CD plastic, GO-HA plastic, and CNT-HA plastic irradiated with artificial solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2).
(F) Photothermal conversion efficiency of P-CD plastic compared with previously reported photothermal materials, including inorganic materials and organic materials.

(G) A radar image of comparison of leakage, tensile strength, toughness, temperature increase, and photothermal conversion efficiency between P-CD plastic, GO-HA plastic, and CNT-HA plastic. The content of photothermal reagent was 20% for all samples.

(H) Temperature changes of P-CD plastic for repeated cycles of switching on and off simulated solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2) after storage for 1 month, UV aging for 32 h, and immersion in aqueous ethanol (1:1, v/v) for 12 h. See also Figures S15–S18 and Scheme S5.
Ultra-stable, highly sensitive, and efficient TEGs based on P-CD plastics
(A and B) Digital images demonstrating (A) the design of solar-powered TEGs and (B) setup of solar-powered TEGs upon solar irradiation.
(C) Voltage generation using solar-powered TEGs under various intensities of light irradiation.
(D) Cyclical voltage generation and disappearance of solar-powered TEGs when the simulated solar source (100 mW cm−2) was switched on and off cyclically.
(E) Voltage generation and disappearance of solar-powered TEGs under mimetic all-weather conditions (solar source = 100 mW cm−2). See also Figures S19–S22 and Video S1.

head.ce:title: Constructing ultra-stable photothermal plastics assisted by carbon dots with photocaged reactivity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal materials, especially photothermal plastics, are crucial building blocks for functional devices. Covalently immobilizing the photothermal carbon dots in plastic matrix is a promising method for producing efficient photothermal plastics. However, the reactive moieties of photothermal carbon dots are often destroyed because of harsh preparation conditions, preventing their covalent interaction with plastic matrix. Here, we conceptualized carbon dots with photocaged reactivity (P-CDs) for producing ultra-stable photothermal plastics. During the formation of P-CDs, hydroxyl moieties were maintained in the preparation environment. Upon UV irradiation, hydroxyl moieties of P-CDs were in situ converted into aldehyde groups and reacted with amino groups in the polysaccharide matrix, producing P-CD plastics. As-obtained P-CD plastics showed strong stability against solution immersion and UV aging. In particular, the P-CD plastics showed a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 46.6%. Such efficient and robust photothermal P-CD plastics were further applied to prepare a solar-driven thermoelectric generator (TEG) for energy generation.

Photothermal materials, especially photothermal plastics, are crucial building blocks in energy science, agriculture, and bio-medical applications. Covalently immobilizing the photothermal carbon dots in plastic matrix is a promising method for producing efficient photothermal plastics. However, the reactive moieties of photothermal carbon dots are often destroyed because of harsh preparation conditions, preventing their covalent interaction with plastic matrix. This work addresses the challenge by developing carbon dots (P-CDs) with photocaged reactivity. The P-CDs maintained stable moieties in the harsh preparation process, while reactive moieties, which can form covalent bond with plastic matrix, were in situ generated upon light irradiation. In such a manner, efficient and robust photothermal P-CD plastics were obtained. More interestingly, P-CD plastics were successfully employed for constructing a solar-driven thermoelectric generator (TEG) for energy generation.

Highly reactive photothermal carbon dots are promising building blocks for functional plastics but remain challenging. Here, we present carbon dots with photocaged reactivity (P-CDs) for producing robust solar-thermal plastics. Stable P-CDs released reactive aldehyde upon light irradiation, which efficiently reacted with amino groups in the plastic matrix. As a result, P-CD plastics showed superior stability, ultra-robustness, and a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 46.6%. The P-CD plastics were employed for efficient solar-thermal-electricity conversion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: This study did not generate or analyze (datasets or code).

ce:section.ce:para:
Photothermal plastics, i.e., plastics that convert photons into thermal energy, show huge potential for self-healing materials, smart devices, and actuators. 1–4 Photothermal plastics are typically prepared by physically mixing photothermal reagents, such as organic dyes and nanoparticles, in a plastic matrix. 3
,
5 Although substantial progress has been achieved in this field, several challenges remain. As most photothermal plastics are produced by physical mixing, they suffer from the problem of the leakage of photothermal reagents from the matrix. In addition, organic photothermal plastics are generally prone to photobleaching. These challenges hinder their practical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) are optical carbon nanomaterials that have sizes below 10 nm. 6
,
7 CDs were first discovered serendipitously in 2004 as an impurity in the synthesis of single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and were purified by preparative electrophoresis. 8 In 2006, Sun et al. reported the preparation of luminescent CDs using laser ablation and surface passivation. 9 Since then, rapidly increasing numbers of studies on CDs have been carried out, as CDs can be easily prepared from readily available substances using simple synthetic routes and they have excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. 10–13 Moreover, CDs also showed stable and efficient photothermal conversion properties. 14
,
15 Ideally, covalently immobilized CDs in plastic matrix would be an effective method for fabricating ultra-stable, highly efficient, and robust photothermal plastics, addressing the above-mentioned challenges. Nevertheless, realization of the goal was hindered by the lower reactivity of photothermal CDs. This is because most of photothermal CDs were prepared under conditions with high temperature and high pressure, 13 where reactive moieties would be easily destroyed. To this end, attention was paid to photocages. Photocages are general tools in biological chemistry. The activity of functional molecules can be “caged” by photosensitive molecules (photocages). 16
,
17 Under UV irradiation, the photocage releases the target substance and activates its function. Here, we used vanillin to prepare 4-(4-(hydroxymethyl)-2-methoxy-5-nitrophenoxy) butanoic acid and further produce carbon dots with photocaged reactivity (P-CDs). As-prepared P-CDs showed photocaged reactivity (Figure 1A). Specifically, P-CDs were stable with surface hydroxyl moieties that can be transformed into reactive aldehyde moieties upon UV irradiation. Ultra-stable robust P-CD plastics were prepared by irradiating a mixture of P-CDs and amino-functionalized hyaluronic acid (HA-CDH) with UV light, which triggered an “aldehyde-amine” crosslinking reaction between P-CDs and the matrix (Figure 1B). The obtained P-CD plastics were extremely robust, and no leakage of P-CDs was observed when the plastics were immersed in aqueous solution. The P-CD plastics demonstrate high photothermal efficiency, which remained stable after long-time storage, UV aging, and treatments in solutions. As a demonstration of potential applications, as-developed photothermal P-CD plastics were successfully used to construct a solar-driven TEG (Figure 1C).

The precursor molecule, 4-(4-(hydroxymethyl)-2-methoxy-5-nitrophenoxy) butanoic acid, was synthesized from vanillin as described in previous reports. 16
,
18 The precursor molecule and 1,10-diaminodecane were then dissolved in ethanol and transferred to a high-pressure reactor. After the reaction, the system was cooled to room temperature to allow the collection of the resulting P-CDs (Figure 1A). The microstructure of the P-CDs was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD). A characteristic diffraction peak was observed at around 23° (Figure S1), which is attributed to the (002) facet of graphite by comparison with the standard JCPDS card (no. 41-1487). This sharp peak represents a high degree of graphitization of the P-CDs. A broad peak was also observed at 20°–26°, which suggests the presence of some amorphous P-CDs. The crystal distance of the as-prepared P-CDs was measured to be around 0.12 nm via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Figure 2A). Dynamic light scattering analysis showed that P-CDs had an average size of ∼3.7 nm (Figure S2). To ensure the reproducibility, P-CDs were prepared in five batches under equal conditions and all of them showed similar sizes (Figure S2).

According to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the P-CDs contain C (∼75.17%), N (∼2.07%), and O (∼22.76%) (Figure 2B). The abundant oxygen contents should mainly originate because: 19
,
20 (1) P-CDs were prepared in ethanol as solvent at high temperature and pressure, indicating that the surface would be easily oxidized and generate lots of oxygen atoms. (2) The precursor molecules contained hydroxyl moieties and P-CDs inherited these moieties from the precursor molecules. High-resolution XPS measurements on elemental C, O, and N showed the presence of C=O, C–O, and O–H moieties. The C1s spectrum showed four peaks corresponding to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), C–N/C–N–C (285.1eV), and C=O (289.1 eV) linkages (Figure S3A). The O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three doublets corresponding to C–O/O–H (531.7 eV) and O=C–O (532.9 eV) linkages (Figure S3B). The C–N–C signal in the N1s spectrum suggests the formation of amide moieties (Figure S3C) by reaction of carboxylic acid and amine groups during the solvothermal preparation of P-CDs. In agreement with the XPS analysis, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum also demonstrated the presence of –NH2 (3,528 cm−1), –OH (3,269 cm−1), and –O-C=O (1,710 cm−1) moieties in the P-CDs (Figure S4).

Having determined the morphology and structure of the P-CDs, we next investigated their photosensitivity. The absorbance peak of the P-CDs at 350 nm was red shifted to 375 nm upon UV irradiation for 80 s (Figure 2C), which is similar to the shift observed for 4-(4-(hydroxymethyl)-2-methoxy-5-nitrophenoxy) butanoic acid, the precursor molecule (Figure S5). Generally, the hydroxyl moieties of such precursor molecule converted to aldehyde moieties upon UV irradiation (Scheme S1). 21 As a result, we speculated that a photoreaction similar to that of the photocage should occur in P-CDs. A comparison of 1H NMR spectra between P-CDs without/with UV irradiation also suggests that aldehyde moieties (signal at 9.65 ppm) appeared after UV irradiation, which was similar to the precursor molecule (Figure S6). We further used the FT-IR spectra of P-CDs before and after UV irradiation to verify the generation of aldehyde moieties. A pronounced signal at 1,701 cm−1, attributed to aldehyde groups, 22 appeared in the FT-IR spectrum after UV irradiation (Figure 2D), confirming the in situ generation of aldehyde moieties upon UV irradiation of P-CDs. According to the photo-kinetic curve of P-CDs (Figure 2E), no hydroxyl-to-aldehyde conversion was observed without UV irradiation. However, the in situ generation of aldehyde groups increased upon increased UV irradiation. The aldehyde content (ALD %) of P-CDs went up to 89.3% upon UV irradiation for 20 min (Figure 2E). The P-CDs also showed highly increased absorbance upon irradiation over several cycles, with no change in absorbance when the UV light source was removed (Figure S7). The in situ aldehyde-amine coupling reaction of P-CDs with carbohydrazide (CDH) was also confirmed by 1H NMR spectroscopy (Scheme S2; Figure S8). Therefore, P-CDs demonstrate excellent photo controllability and aldehyde groups can be generated as required.

Photothermal conversion of the P-CDs was further investigated using simulated solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2, 20 min). The temperature of irradiated P-CDs increased from 32.2°C to 57.1°C with a photothermal conversion efficiency of 33.2%, whereas the temperature of un-irradiated P-CDs only increased from 31.1°C to 38.5°C with a photothermal conversion efficiency of 9.8% (Figure 2F). More interestingly, different exposure time of P-CDs to UV sources resulted in different photothermal conversion. Specifically, P-CDs demonstrated a temperature increase from ∼7.4°C to ∼24.9°C when the exposure to UV light went up from 0 to 20 min (Figure S9). This difference is partially attributed to the enhanced absorbance of P-CDs after UV irradiation, as shown in Figure S10. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of P-CDs after UV irradiation was much lower than that before UV irradiation (Figure 2G), suggesting that radiative migration of excited state P-CDs after UV irradiation was greatly suppressed. In addition, the suppressed radiative migration was also confirmed by decreased fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of P-CDs after UV irradiation (Figure S11). As a result, the non-radiative migration of excited states of P-CDs was enhanced after UV irradiation (Figure 2H). Taking all these results together, the enhanced photothermal conversion of P-CDs was as follows: the absorbance range of P-CD powders in the visible region was strongly widened after hydroxyl-to-aldehyde conversion, indicating that P-CDs can absorb and utilize solar photons more efficiently. After P-CDs absorbed the solar photons, the energy was consumed in non-radiative migration and boosted photothermal conversion. All of these results demonstrate the readily high in situ reactivity and excellent photothermal conversion of P-CDs under UV irradiation. Such P-CDs, with the in situ formation of aldehyde groups, can thus be promising for the preparation of robust photothermal plastics.

P-CD plastics were further prepared by mixing P-CDs with HA-CDH. Specifically, P-CDs were dispersed in an aqueous solution of HA-CDH, where HA-CDH was prepared as described previously (Scheme S3). 22 The mixture was then exposed to 375 nm UV light to trigger the in situ formation of aldehyde groups on the P-CDs, which then reacted with the amine groups in HA-CDH. A new peak at 1,559 cm−1 assigned to C=N moieties appeared in the FT-IR spectra after 20 min irradiation (Figures S12A and S12B), verifying the expected covalent crosslinking reaction. P-CD plastics were obtained by drying the crosslinked mixture at room temperature (Figure 3A). The whole process was reproducible since P-CD plastic made in five batches showed similar FT-IR spectra (Figure S12B). According to atomic force microscopy (AFM), the P-CD plastics showed a rough surface morphology demonstrating small aggregates of P-CDs with a length of 3–3.2 μm and a width of 1–2 μm (Figure 3B). Such rough morphology could be caused by P-CDs interacting with HA-CDH chains during the photo-triggered crosslinking of aldehyde and amine groups. Moreover, such rough surfaces are beneficial for light management and positively contribute to the ensuing photothermal conversion. 23

To investigate the mechanical performance of P-CD plastics, two plastics (non-crosslinked P-CD plastic and control plastic as shown in Figures 3C and 3D, respectively) were also prepared for comparison. Stress-strain measurements of P-CD plastics showed that the composition of the P-CDs and photo-triggered covalent crosslinking greatly affected their mechanical performances. Non-doped HA-CDH plastic (control plastic) showed an average tensile strength of ∼28.4 MPa and an average strain to failure of ∼3.0% (Figures 3E and 3F). Non-crosslinked P-CD plastic with the sole addition of P-CDs in the plastics without UV irradiation showed an average tensile strength of ∼59.3 MPa and a strain to failure of ∼3.8% (Figures 3E and 3F). These results demonstrate that the simple addition of P-CDs can enhance the non-covalent interactions in the matrix and thus improve its tensile strength. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to investigate this effect (Scheme S4; Figure S13). The models showed that many more hydrogen bonds were formed between added P-CDs and HA-CDH chains, with a ratio of HA-CDH chains/P-CDs of 2:1 (of about 10 hydrogen bonds within the simulation model), compared with only about 3 hydrogen bonds between HA-CDH chains within the simulation model (Figure S13). The increase of the number of P-CDs led to more hydrogen bonds (∼15) with a ratio of HA-CDH chains/P-CDs of 1:2. The simulated binding energy between HA-CDH chains and P-CDs (∼−4.3 eV) was also higher than that between HA-CDH chains (∼−1.2 eV) (Table S1).

After the photo-crosslinking of P-CDs with the plastic matrix, the tensile strength and strain to failure of the P-CD plastics further increased to ∼124 MPa and ∼10.1%, respectively (Figures 3E and 3F). P-CD plastics also showed higher toughness (7.0 MJ cm−3) than the control plastic (0.3 MJ cm−3) and non-crosslinked P-CD plastic (1.2 MJ cm−3) (Figure 3G). In addition, P-CD plastics showed a Young’s modulus of ∼1,900 MPa, which was doubled in value compared with control plastic (Figure 3H). Notably, P-CD plastics possessed superior tensile strengths and Young’s moduli in comparison with other widely used plastics (Figure 3I). 24 The curve of dissipation factor (tan δ) of the non-crosslinked P-CD plastic showed a linear increase between 40°C and 114°C with a glass transition temperature of 113.4°C, which indicates steady softening of the composite plastics (Figure S14). In comparison, crosslinked P-CD plastics showed softening behaviors in the range of 160°C–190°C with a glass transition temperature of 187.4°C (Figure S14). Therefore, the photo-crosslinking reaction induced by UV irradiation strongly increased both the mechanical strength and the thermal stability of P-CD plastics.

At the same time, the P-CDs crosslinked in the plastic matrix can effectively prevent the leakage problem of photothermal agent. According to UV-vis spectroscopy, no absorbance signals of P-CDs was observed in the aqueous ethanol after treating the crosslinked plastics over 70 h, indicating no detectable leakage of P-CDs (Figure 3J). In contrast, the aqueous ethanol after treating non-crosslinked P-CD plastics over 70 h showed obvious absorbance signals for P-CDs, indicating the leakage of P-CDs from the matrix of non-crosslinked P-CD plastic (Figure 3J). Furthermore, photothermal plastics were also made via mixing the most general photothermal reagents, including graphene oxide (GO) and CNTs, with the HA-CDH matrix. The resultant GO-HA plastic and CNT-HA plastic showed obvious leakage after immersion in water (Figure 3J). All these results demonstrate the success of our rational strategy via UV light irradiation during the preparation process to increase both the mechanical performance and thermal stability of the polysaccharide-based plastics, as well as to prevent the leakage of the photothermal reagents from the plastics matrix.

Previous results showed that aldehyde moieties were crucial for the photothermal conversion of P-CDs. However, aldehyde moieties of P-CDs changed to –C=N moieties when P-CD plastics were formed. The effect of such moieties on the photothermal performance should be studied first. To this end, P-CDs powders with different ratios of –CHO and –C=N were prepared. Excitingly, the temperature increase of P-CD powders was even slightly enhanced (from ∼24.9°C to 28.1°C) with increasing ratio between –C=N and –CHO moieties, indicating improved photothermal efficiency (Figure S15). This might be attributed to the further promoted non-radiative migration caused by –C=N moieties. 25
,
26 This result encouraged us to further investigate the photothermal conversion of P-CD plastics. The absorbance spectra were first measured as this is a crucial parameter for photothermal conversion (Figure 4A). P-CD plastic showed strong absorbance over a wide range of wavelengths (200–2,600 nm), which overlapped well with the solar spectrum. This result indicated the potential of these plastics as a solar absorber for photothermal conversion (Figure 4A). Notably, P-CD plastics showed higher absorbance than non-crosslinked P-CD plastic, which is consistent with the observed UV-triggered absorbance enhancement of P-CDs in aqueous solutions. Photothermal conversion by the P-CD plastics was investigated by exposing the plastics to standard 1 sun (100 mW cm−2) irradiation. The temperature of the P-CD plastics increased from ∼24.3°C to ∼59.5°C after 20 min of 1 sun irradiation (Figure 4B). Moreover, P-CD plastic made in five batches showed reproducible photothermal conversion (Figure S16). For comparison, non-crosslinked P-CD plastic and control plastic demonstrated only a slight temperature increase under equal conditions (Figure 4B). This higher photothermal conversion of P-CD plastics should be attributed to the higher absorbance coefficient and suppressed radiative migration of P-CDs triggered by UV light during the preparation process. Photothermal conversion of P-CD plastics was further studied under different simulated solar intensities. The exposure of plastics to 2 and 3 standard sun irradiation led to temperature increase to ∼64.5°C and ∼95.8°C, respectively, demonstrating their efficient photothermal conversion (Figure 4C). The influence of P-CD concentration on the photothermal conversion of plastics was also investigated. The temperature went higher with the higher concentration of P-CDs in the plastic. P-CD plastic showed a temperature increase of 59.5°C when the fraction of P-CDs went to 20% (w/w) (Figure 4D). The temperature increase reached equilibrium upon further increasing the fraction of P-CDs to 30% (w/w) (Figure 4D). As a comparison, the photothermal conversion of GO-HA plastic (20% GO) and CNT-HA (20% CNT) plastic were also studied. P-CD plastic showed an ∼35.7°C temperature increase, which was higher than CNT-HA plastic (∼26.2°C) and GO-HA plastic (∼33.1°C) (Figure 4E). More interestingly, this photothermal efficiency of P-CD plastics (46.6%) was higher than most reported organic-derived photothermal materials, such as Phth-PEG, 27 Chl-PMMA, 27 HB-PEG, 28 Chlin-PEG, 28 and Lignin-PVA 29 (Figure 4F). Moreover, it also surpassed the photothermal efficiency of inorganic/complexed materials, such as GnPs-ER, 30 LBF-PVA, 31 LS-CuS, 32 and MP-PC 33 (Figure 4F).

To further highlight the superiority of P-CD plastic, the fundamental statistics of leakage, tensile strength, toughness, maximum photothermal temperature, and photothermal conversion efficiency between P-CD plastic, GO-HA plastic, and CNT-HA plastic were compared (Figures 4G and S17). P-CD plastic showed comprehensive advantages, compared with these photothermal plastics. Furthermore, P-CD plastics showed remarkable photothermal stability. After storage for 1 month, UV aging for 32 h, and immersion in aqueous ethanol for 12 h (followed by drying at 40°C for 40 min before the measurement), P-CD plastics still showed quick solar-to-thermal response and maintained a photothermal conversion efficiency of ∼46.6% for tens of cycles (Figure 4H). All these results demonstrated that P-CD plastics had high and stable photothermal conversion efficiency as well as superior stability for applications in multiple cycles. In addition, P-CDs were mixed with other sustainable matrixes, including amino-modified carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC-CDH) and chitosan (CS), to produce photothermal plastics. Here, CMC-CDH was prepared according to previous work (Scheme S5). 29 P-CD plastics made from CMC (CMC-P-CD plastic) and CS (CS-P-CD plastic) showed temperature increases of ∼33.1°C and 36.8°C upon solar irradiation for 20 min, respectively (Figure S18). Moreover, the tensile strength of the P-CD plastics made from CMC (CMC-P-CD plastic) and CS (CS-P-CD plastic) were ∼110.3 and ∼128.6 MPa, respectively (Figure S18). These results demonstrated that as-developed P-CDs had good generality for producing photothermal plastics together with a wide range of polysaccharide matrixes.

Encouraged by these results, we further used P-CD plastics to fabricate a solar-driven TEG, which could directly convert photothermal energy into electrical energy via the Seebeck effect. 34 TEGs driven by photothermal materials provide an attractive option for solar energy harvesting based on their particular advantages, such as having no moving parts, requiring little maintenance, and delivering a long lifespan of service without noise.

We constructed a TEG with P-CD plastic as shown in Figures 5A and 5B. The P-CD plastic absorbed solar light and heated the thermoelectric module on one side of the device. The temperature difference between the heated P-CD plastic side and the other side near the cooling system of the device generated voltages of ∼110 and ∼240 mV after 25 min of 1 and 3 sun irradiation, respectively (Figure 5C). Switching off the light source immediately reduced the voltage generated by the TEG (Figure 5D), demonstrating the good controllability, rapid response, and high sensitivity of the device. Moreover, a portable solar-driven TEG can be achieved by employing PMMA hydrogel as a cooling layer (Figures S19 and S20). Importantly, we successfully applied the solar-driven TEG to power the rotation of a fan (Figure 5B; Video S1). Therefore, P-CD plastics can be used for efficient energy generation for practical purposes. The solar-driven TEGs constructed with P-CD plastics were evaluated in mimetic all-weather conditions (Figures 5E, S21, and S22). The device showed stable electricity generation with a surficial temperature of ∼44.4°C during the mimetic sunny days. The output voltage (∼100 mV) dramatically decreased under the mimetic rainy weather (Figures 5E, S21, and S22A). After the natural evaporation of the accumulated water on the device, the output voltage recovered with a stable surficial temperature of ∼44.7°C upon solar irradiation (Figures 5E, S21, and S22B). Subsequently, the device was further investigated under mimetic frozen weather. The output voltage of the TEG device dramatically decreased upon ice treatment, and recovered quickly under mimetic solar irradiation (Figures 5E, S21, and S22C). These results showed the ultra-stability, rapid recoverability, and high sensitivity of solar-driven TEGs based on P-CD plastics under distinct weather conditions, which allows the performance of TEGs to swiftly and efficiently, reversibly, and rapidly change for multiple cycles between distinct conditions. These excellent performances of TEGs verified the superior properties of P-CD plastics and the successful application of P-CD plastics for TEGs.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Video S1. Setup of solar-powered TEGs upon solar irradiation

ce:section.ce:para:
In summary, we developed a robust photothermal plastic using P-CDs with photocaged reactivity. The in situ hydroxyl-to-aldehyde conversion under UV irradiation allowed the coupling of P-CDs with amine groups in a polysaccharide-based HA-CDH plastic matrix. This covalent bonding prevented the leakage of photothermal reagents from the plastic matrix, which often occurred in reported photothermal plastics. As an extra benefit, the crosslinking of the P-CDs with the polysaccharide chains via an aldehyde-amine reaction enhanced both the mechanical performance and thermal stability of the obtained photothermal plastics. The P-CD plastics showed high photothermal efficiency of 46.6% and excellent photothermal stability. The high photostability enabled the plastic to be used for extended periods of time. Furthermore, P-CD plastics were successfully used to construct a solar-driven TEG, which was extremely stable in all-weather conditions. Considering their robustness, solar-driven TEGs based on P-CD plastics are promising for energy output under various weather conditions in real applications. Moreover, considering the great potential of photothermal materials in bio-medical applications, P-CD plastic could be further processed into functional materials for anti-cancer therapy and wound healing. 35
,
36 In a broader view, as-developed P-CDs with photocaged reactivity might not only be used for fabricating photothermal plastics but also applied for preparing efficient and robust solar-thermal devices for solar steam generation, 37 as photocatalysts, 38 and for phase change energy storage together with various amino-incorporated molecular matrix. 39
,
40
Further information and requests for resources and materials should be directed to, and will be fulfilled by, the lead contact, Zhijun Chen (chenzhijun@nefu.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new unique materials.

HA, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and trifluoroacetic acid were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Vanillin was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai). CDH was purchased from 9 Ding Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). 1,10-Diaminodecane was purchased from Bidepharm (Shanghai, China). Methyl 4-bromobutyrate and CS were bought from Aladdin Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). GO solution (1 mg mL) was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent. Carboxylic single-walled CNT dispersion (0.15 wt %) was purchased from Macklin. N,N′-Methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBAA), ammonium persulfate (APS), acrylamide (AAM), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEDA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent. All other solvents (HNO3, DMF, DCM, THF, and CH3OH, etc.) were supplied by Tianli Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). Deionized water, prepared using a Master Q30UT water purification system (Hitech Instruments, Shanghai, China), was used for all experiments. The thermoelectric module, motor, and water-cooled sheet were purchased from Alibaba (Hangzhou, China). Temperatures recorded in photothermal conversion calculation were carried out using a DTM-180A digital thermometer (Zhaohui Instruments, Hengshui, China).

1H NMR spectra were recorded using an Avance III HD 500 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). Dimethylsulfoxide-d6 (DMSO-d6) was used as the solvent and 1H NMR chemical shifts were referenced to the DMSO signals. Scanning electron microscopy images were obtained using a GeminiSEM 300 field emission scanning electron microscope (ZEISS, Germany). TEM images were obtained using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Absorption spectra were recorded over the range of 200–2,600 nm, using a PE Lambda 750 UV/vis/NIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Germany). XRD patterns were collected using an X’Pert3 Powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical, Malvern, UK). The XRD analysis was performed at a scanning speed of 5°/s, a scanning step size of 0.02°, and a 2θ range of 5°–85°. Photo-luminescence spectra were recorded using a Fluoromax 4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Instruments, Edison, NJ, USA). The surface topography of the films was analyzed using statistic studies of AFM images, obtained using a Bruker D3100 atomic force microscope (Bruker France, Palaiseau, France). FT-IR spectra were collected using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. Samples were scanned over the range of 550–4,000 cm−1, with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The content of aldehyde groups (ALD %) was used to investigate conversion efficiency, where hydroxy moieties change into aldehyde moieties upon UV light irradiation. The route of determination of the ALD % came from previous work. 41 Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was carried out using a dynamic mechanical analyzer (TA Instruments, DMA 2980) in tension mode. Rectangular samples (5 cm × 1 cm × 0.06 mm) were used, and the distance between the clamps was 20 mm. The analysis was carried out at a frequency of 1 Hz from 0°C to 200°C, at a heating rate of 5°C min−1. Glass transition temperatures were estimated from the temperature of the maximum value of loss factor (tan δ). Tensile performance was tested using rectangular-shaped samples (5 cm × 1 cm × 0.06 mm, gauge length 20 mm) at room temperature. Uniaxial tensile measurements were carried out using an Electronic Universal Testing Machine (Suns Technology Stock, Shenzhen, China), with an extension rate of 5 mm/min. Infrared images were recorded using a Testo 869 thermal imaging camera (Testo, Lenzkirch, Germany). A CEL-S500 xenon lamp (Au-light, Beijing, China), which simulates solar irradiation, was used as the light source. Changes in voltage were recorded using a Keithley 6514 system electrometer (Keithley, USA). In all cases, the UV source was a 375 nm LED lamp (10 mW cm−2) and the distance between the light and solution was 5 cm. Artificial solar light was a xenon lamp (standard 1 sun, 100 mW cm−2; 2 sun, 200 mW cm−2; and 3 sun, 300 mW cm−2).

A solution of precursor molecule (0.3 g, 1 mmol) and 1,10-diaminodecane (0.057 g, 0.3 mmol) in ethanol (40 mL) were reacted at 180°C for 8 h in a high-pressure reactor. After cooling naturally to room temperature, P-CDs were obtained by evaporating the solvent.

EDC (0.96 g, 5.0 mmol) was first added to 100 g of 1 wt % CMC solution and magnetically stirred for 1 h. NHS (0.58 g, 5.0 mmol) and CDH (0.45 g, 5.0 mmol) were then added. After that, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 5 by adding 0.1 M HCl. The reaction was stirred for 24 h at room temperature under N2 atmosphere. The reaction mixture was then dialyzed for 5 days in deionized water. After dialysis, the resulting CDH-grafted CMC (CMC-CDH) dispersion was dried in the oven at 40°C overnight (Scheme S5).

A solution of HA-CDH (1 g) in deionized water (100 mL) was stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous. The solution was ultrasonicated for a few minutes to ensure complete dissolution, then poured into a Teflon mold (10 × 6 × 2 cm) or glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). The solution was kept at room temperature for a few days to remove water and obtain the control plastics.

A solution of HA-CDH (1 g) in deionized water (100 mL) was stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous. The solution was then ultrasonicated for a few minutes to ensure complete dissolution. A solution of P-CDs in ethanol (1 g L−1) was added to the aqueous solution of HA-CDH and the mixed solution was then poured into a Teflon mold (10 × 6 × 2 cm) or glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). The solution was kept at room temperature for a few days to remove water and obtain the non-crosslinked P-CD plastics. Notably, the P-CD content in non-crosslinked P-CD plastics was 20%.

A solution of HA-CDH (1 g) in deionized water (100 mL) was stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous. The solution was then ultrasonicated for a few minutes to ensure complete dissolution. A solution of P-CDs in ethanol (1 g L−1) was added to the aqueous solution of HA-CDH and the mixed solution was then poured into a Teflon mold (10 × 6 × 2 cm) or glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). The aqueous solutions containing HA-CDH and P-CD nanoparticles were irradiated for 20 min with 375 nm UV (10 mW cm−2). The distance between the light and the solution was ∼5 cm. Finally, the solution was kept at room temperature for a few days to remove water and obtain P-CD plastics. Notably, different concentration of P-CDs (0.5%, 1%, 2%, 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25%, and 30%) were used to prepare P-CD plastic.

A solution of HA-CDH (1 g) in deionized water (100 mL) was stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous and the solution was then ultrasonicated for a few minutes to ensure complete dissolution. A solution of GO solution (1 mg mL−1) was added to the aqueous solution of HA-CDH and the mixed solution was then poured into a glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). These solutions were kept at room temperature for a few days to remove water and obtain the GO-HA plastic. Notably, the GO content in GO-HA plastic was 20%.

A solution of HA-CDH (1 g) in deionized water (100 mL) was stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous and the solution was then ultrasonicated for a few minutes to ensure complete dissolution. A solution of carboxylic single-walled CNT dispersion was added to the aqueous solution of HA-CDH and the mixed solution was then poured into a glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). These solutions were kept at room temperature for a few days to remove water and obtain the CNT-HA plastic. The CNT content in CNT-HA plastic was 20%.

A solution of CMC-CDH (1 g) in deionized water (100 mL) was stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous. A solution of P-CDs in ethanol (1 g L−1) was added to the aqueous solution of CMC-CDH and the mixed solution was then poured into a Teflon mold (10 × 6 × 2 cm) or glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). The aqueous solutions containing CMC-CDH and P-CDs were irradiated for 20 min with 375 nm UV (10 mW cm−2). The distance between the light and the solution was ∼5 cm. Finally, the solution was kept at room temperature for water evaporation to give CMC-P-CD plastics. Notably, the P-CD content in CMC-P-CD plastic was 20%.

A solution of CS (1 g) was added into deionized water (100 mL). Then, the pH value of the solution was adjusted to ∼4, and stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous. A solution of P-CDs in ethanol (1 g L−1) was added to the aqueous solution of CS and the mixed solution was then poured into a Teflon mold (10 × 6 × 2 cm) or glass mold (10 × 10 × 3 cm). The aqueous solutions containing CS and P-CDs were irradiated for 20 min with 375 nm UV (10 mW cm−2). The distance between the light and the solution was ∼5 cm. Finally, the solution was kept at room temperature for water evaporation to give a plastic. As-obtained plastic was then immersed in NaOH solution (pH 8) for deprotonation, washed with water, and dried under room temperature to give CS-P-CD plastics. Notably, the P-CD content in CS-P-CD plastic was 20%.

A solution of AAM (16.224 g), APS (0.096 g), and MBAA (0.016 g) was dissolved in deionized water (120 mL) and stirred continuously until the solution was homogeneous at room temperature. Then, TEDA (60 μL) was added promptly. The aqueous solutions containing AAM, APS, MBAA, and TEDA were irradiated for a few minutes to form PMMA hydrogel (10 ×10 × 1 cm) with 375 nm UV (10 mW cm−2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the ratio of aldehyde groups (–CHO) and imine groups (–C=N)
ce:section.ce:para: Exposure of P-CDs to UV (375 nm) for 20 min led to a maximum hydroxyl-to-aldehyde conversion (89.3%). The maximum value was defined as 100%. Then, CDH (0.3 g) was added to P-CD (–CHO, 0.15 g) solution (10 mL, ethanol/water = 1/4) for reaction at room temperature (Scheme S2). After a periodic time, the reaction mixture was measured for the amount of –CHO. 41 Considering the almost quantitative conversion of such an aldehyde-amine reaction, the as-generated amount of –C=N was approximately equal to the decreased value of –CHO. As a result, the –C=N/–CHO ratio was obtained.

DFT calculations were performed using the Dmol3 software package. 42 The exchange-correlation interaction was treated using the generalized gradient approximation with the PBE functional. 43 A double numerical quality basis set with d-type polarization function was used for all geometric optimizations and total energy calculations. 44 The core electrons were modeled using the effective core pseudopotentials of Dolg and co-workers. 45
,
46 Grimme’s semi-empirical DFT-D was introduced in the computations to guarantee a better description of long-range electron interactions. 47

All calculations were performed spin unrestricted. The real space cutoff radius was 0.37 nm. The convergence tolerance for SCF was 1.0 × 10−6 Ha and those for geometry optimization of energy and maximum force were 1.0 × 10−5 Ha and 0.002 Ha/Å, respectively. The binding energy (E b) is defined as
(Equation 1)
E b
=
E
A + B
−
E A
−
E B
,
where E A+B is the total energy of combined A and B, and E B + E A is the sum of the total energies of A and B before combination.
P-CD powder (100 mg) was irradiated with artificial solar light (100 mW cm−2). The temperature change of P-CD powder was recorded using an IR camera.

Photothermal conversion efficiency was carried out in an aqueous solution of P-CDs after UV irradiation for 20 min (1 mg mL−1, 2 mL), or a piece of P-CD plastic (15 mg) in a vessel with an insulating layer and illuminating the aqueous solution with artificial solar light (100 mW cm−2). The temperature of the solution was recorded using a digital thermometer, and the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was estimated following previous publications: 31
(Equation 2)
η =
Q E
=
C m Δ T
P s t
=
C ρ V Δ T
P S t
,
where Q is the thermal energy generated and E is the total energy of incident light. Q is determined by the heat capacity (C), density (ρ), volume (V), and ΔT over 10 min irradiation of the solution. E is determined by the power of the incident light (P), irradiation area (S), and irradiation time (t). The values of C (4.2 J g−1 °C−1), S (4 cm2), and ρ (1 g cm−3) for water were used in calculations.
Sunny day: the mimetic solar source was on.

Rainy day and natural evaporation of accumulated water: the mimetic solar source was first switched off and water was subsequently added on the surface of the device for natural evaporation without solar irradiation. After no obvious water was observed on the top of the device, the solar source was switched on again.

Rainy day and evaporation upon solar irradiation: the mimetic solar source was first switched off and water was subsequently added on the surface of the device. After that, the solar source was switched on immediately.
Frozen day: the mimetic solar source was first switched off and ice was subsequently added on the surface of the device. After that, the solar source was switched on immediately.
